name: Ball, Laura Lee
description: One afternoon in the fall of 1917, a sad faced, disappointed-looking girl was seen sitting in one corner of the lobby of Hattiesburg Hall. This young person was our Laura Lee. Soon, however, the disappointed look passed away as Laura Lee made friends and became interested in her studies, especially "Plant Life." She was so interested in this particular study that she spent all her recreation periods strolling on the campus. We feel that this particular interest may have a very great influence on Laura Lee's future.

name: Barnett, Una Mildred
description: At the home of Dr. and Mrs. Barnett, Mildred became known as "baby of the family." Before entering school, she spent an unusual amount of time playing. Oftentimes when asked to do anything other than play, she would reply, "In a little bit." This expression is still used by her on many and various occasions. Through the little home school and high school at Carthage, Mildred was very patient and studious. In college she has been very quiet and reserved. When asked how she played varsity basketball each of the three years in college, took music, held different offices in student organizations, and then made such a splendid record in class work, she said: "Oh, anybody can do that well." Mildred's college friends regret that she is leaving, still each one is glad that she has finished and takes with her fond memories, lasting hopes, and high ideals.

name: Broome, Mattie Mae
description: If you want to know anything about the Post Office, ask Mattie Mae. If you can't find her at the moment (the chances are you can't, for if anybody ever kept pace with her or were in as many places at the same time, they were going some), then ask Addie Lou, her roommate, and she'll tell you she's gone to see Mr. Hays. Mattie Mae's eyes fairly laugh at you sometimes. They dance and twinkle as merrily and mischievously as one of Old Nick's little imps. That doesn't mean she's bad. Her goodness has a delightful spice and flavor, but is positive. Here she comes, late to class, but you won't mind for she's been trying to find you for half an hour to give you a Special Delivery letter. Anyway, when she does arrive she makes up for her tardiness by her charming personality—for there cannot be found a more individualistic girl than our own Mattie Mae. No teacher can startle her. She can see quite through the heart of another—we would say that she possesses one of those rare analytical brains which Mr. Hurst sometimes speaks of. You simply have to know her and be with her to find out all her delightful qualities. She takes an active part in college activities.

name: Cox, Bennett Cleveland
motto: "He is a man who dares to do what he thinks is right."
clubs: Secretary and Vice-President, Y. M. C. A.
Representative, Students' International Convention at Des Moines, Iowa
description: After finishing the grammar grades in his home school, Cox attended high school at Ludlow, Mississippi. He then took up the teaching profession and followed it for six years. In September, 1916, Bennett entered the Mississippi Normal College. When the United States declared war on Germany, he went into the army as a private, and after a few months was promoted to sergeant, and served in this capacity for the remaining eighteen months. Cox graduates this session with honors. He has taken an active part in the student organizations; served in these capacities. He is by half an inch the tallest man on the campus. He possesses this distinction by having attained the height of six feet, five and one-half inches.

name: Callihan, Susie
description: Good, reliable Susie, beloved by each and every one. She is a girl constant and true in all things. No one ever went to her for help, no matter how trivial or important it might have been, but that she gave it willingly. Her generosity, lovable character and sweet disposition won for her a place in the hearts of her teachers and schoolmates that shall always be kept just for Susie. She has been described as being "just sweet." We can attribute to her the virtue of wisdom for she has learned her profession in the dear school of experience. Six years has she taught the "tender, young idea how to shoot," and each year finds her more fitted for her labors. As teacher and student, she is a force, not only in the classroom, but in the larger field of daily living.

name: Childress, Margaret
motto: "In full-blown dignity See Wolsey stand, Law in his voice, And fortune in his hand."
description: Thus it was three years ago when a daughter of the Delta, from the banks of the Yazoo joined us. She signed her name Margaret Childress — was she dignified? Some of the new students looked on in awe and thought she was a "faculty." Only a few months had passed when she became a favorite with faculty and students. Even our Camp Shelby friends seemed favorably impressed with her, for — "Maggie is pretty to look at Maggie's a loving lass." Her popularity among the students was attested when she was elected secretary of the Diploma Class, president of the Story Tellers' League. She was also the chairman of the Cheer Leaders and by her pep and ginger helped our eleven to bring home more than one scalp. Our lassie has been a student, too. Each term her name is read out on the "A" list. When we inquire into her future occupation, she is unable to answer, as she hasn't fully decided whether she shall make her specialty Domestic Science and Geometry. We hope when she gets her diploma she will cease to say, "I'm so peeved," but rather "You know, this is wonderful."

name: D'olive, Beulah Mae
description: One of her most striking characteristics is her great love for literature and history. Her life seems to be bubbling over with joy, which she wishes to share with the other students. While she is friendly with all the students in the college, she is the radiant center of a circle of friends whose tastes are congenial with her own, whether they cherish exactly her ideals or not. Wherever she goes, she carries with her an atmosphere of cheerfulness and joy.

name: Ellington, Susie
motto: "All the world's a stage And men and women merely players; They have their exits and entrances, And one man in his time plays many parts."
description: Susie enters the Normal College and captivates the hearts of all by her beauty and charm. She becomes an active member of the Story Tellers' League, Sherwood Bonner Literary Society, Glee Club and Choir; takes a major part in athletics and dramatics. In her last year she is made vice-president of the Student Body. Her popularity increases not only among the girls of her acquaintance but among the boys. All love her and now instead of dignifying her with "Miss Ellington," call her "Snoozie." She answers everything with a hearty "yeah" unless it pertains to "Woods," then she says, "Aw, now." She is never known to study except when going to class, as was noticed when she "pursued on horseback," applied psychology. To psychology you go, The bell invites you, Hear it not "Snoozie," It is a knell That summons you to Heaven or to Hell.

name: Ford, Bettie
motto: "Loyal-hearted, strong of mind, A finer girl nowhere you'll find."
description: Though she is not large in stature, Bettie expects to reach great heights, being one of the most aspiring and ambitious members of our class. She possesses an unlimited amount of patience, energy, loyalty, and, best of all, a "never-give-up" spirit. She can always be depended on to know everything from the minutest detail about the study of English to the most important laws of Psychology. If you want an original idea on anything from a "Society Prophecy" to new views of "Social Hour," go to Bettie. A sufficient proof of her mastery of her work is that she is ever found on the "A" list. She is responsive to all calls, and takes active part in religious work. She is an enthusiastic member of the Hiking Club and Volley Ball team. Although she is a "Ford," she possesses enough dignity to be a very efficient member of the Honor Council. We feel sure that our capable, ambitious Bettie will some day be a very prominent woman, and, in these days of suffrage, there is no telling what position she may some day hold.

name: Fulton, Myrtis
motto: "A more precious gem cannot be found than a sweet girl without a frown."
description: On first acquaintance, one would never guess how much fun Myrtis has about her, for she has the appearance of being very reserved. To her friends, however, Myrtis is a constant source of amusement, for she has a dry wit that makes everyone smile. Her sunniness of disposition and her unfailing good humor win a firm place in the hearts of all who come to know her. Myrtis was graduated from the Noxapater Agricultural High School in the spring of 1917. Deciding upon teaching as a career, she entered the Normal College in September, 1917. Her favorite study was history. She could always find time to read something about the war, no matter how busy.

name: George, J. B.
clubs: Presbyterian Literary Society (President, Vice-President, Secretary, Treasurer)
description: In the person of J. B. George, we have one of Lee County's best products. From a farm near Saltillo, Miss., he came to the Normal College to preserve the greatness of his name, country and state, and to become a factor of the 'Normal College Spirit.' The life of this student before his coming to the Normal College may be given in short by stating that with him came a name more valuable than great riches. As a consequence of possessing the qualities of cheerfulness, sincerity, thoughtfulness, good judgment, conscientiousness, and keenness in discovering his duty, he formed friendships of the lasting type. After spending eleven months in the World War, and, at various times while in school, serving in the capacity of president, vice-president, secretary and treasurer of the Presbyterian Literary Society, member of the Honor Council, member of the Debating Team, chairman of the Bible Study Committee, chairman of the Devotional Committee, and president of the Y. M. C. A., he has demonstrated his character to be one of sterling quality.

name: Hatten, Elsie
motto: "Earnest in purpose, Earnest in thought. She worketh earnestly."
description: This is 'Elsie,' sweet and staid in all of her ways, capable and efficient in her work. Like the Master, her mission seems to be serving her fellow man, and she is faithfully fulfilling it. Many times has the remark been made, 'Go to Elsie Hatten, she will help you,' and no matter the problem, Elsie always has a solution. When circumstances are reversed and she is to receive help, she ponders the matter carefully with the thought, 'Will it look selfish in me to do that?' Not one selfish act can be ascribed to her life among us. So zealously did she perform her duties as president of the Y. W. C. A. that each person in school felt the influence of her strong Christian character. In all her conscientiousness, however, she sometimes announced that prayer meeting for the girls would be held in Forrest County Hall. The general laughter brought forth by the mistake in naming the hall, always made Elsie blush, but everyone knew it was only the twist of the tongue. Elsie is the artist of our class. She revels in the joy of drawing and painting, and if no obstacles arise, she is sure to win distinction for herself in this work.

name: Henderson, Bernice Sigler
motto: "Truth from her lips prevailed with double sway."
description: After journeying around to several places, Bernice travelled down to Mississippi and landed at the Normal College. She resembles another great American in one respect. Her slogan seems to be, "I must never tell a lie." This trait, though troublesome to her friends, has endeared her to their hearts. We cannot miss this "Opp"-ortunity to mention that although Bernice has never made known to any one her ambition, we know that she will go on her "truth-telling way" gladdening hearts with her sweet voice and manner.

name: Hilbun, Bennie
motto: "His heart and hand both open and both free; For what he has, he gives; what he thinks he shows; Yet gives he not 'till judgment guides his bounty."
clubs: President of the Y. M. C. A., 1919-1920
Editor-in-chief of the Normal College News
description: By some of the instructors, Bennie is called "Mr. Hilbun." To his classmates he is known as "Bennie." To the people of Jones county he is "Little Bennie," being smaller than another Bennie of the same name. To the student-body he is the one who stands behind the counter in the book store and takes all the money. But for the money he takes, one gets, not only books and stationery, but the cheer of his genial smile and the charm of his laughing blue eyes. To all Bennie is known as a reliable, helpful person to whom no one ever appeals in vain.

name: Leflore, Jessye
motto: "A smile for all, a greeting glad, An amiable, jolly way she had."
clubs: Member, Y. W. C. A. Cabinet
description: Jessye is recognized by all who know her as one of the most amiable girls who ever attended the Normal College. However, if we should stop with this thought we would only get a narrow insight into the life of this charming young lady. Let us think for a moment of the many student activities into which she entered. She was a faithful student, and diligently pursued all her school work—hence she always enjoyed the pleasurable sensation of hearing her name called on the "A" list. Everyone has a favorite subject—in her case it chanced to be "French," the instructor of which she preferred to be direct from Paris (Miss.). She classified for double periods in this course and was inclined to take it out of class at any convenient time, especially during social hours of Sunday, Tuesday and Saturday evenings.

name: Manton, Gay
motto: "Grace was in all her steps, heaven in her eye. In every gesture dignity and love."
description: She is gentle and kind to everyone, very modest and quiet, earnest in purpose, and conscientious in her work. In fact, Gay entered every college activity with zest—save that of "Campus Course." And we, who know her best, are certain that letters from a "young Prof. back home," explain this seeming indifference. After completing her college course at the close of the third term of this session, Gay went to West Point—well, she said, to teach school.

name: Merkel, J. B.
description: This reference is to be of one of the most likeable and dependable members of our class. When Mrs. Merkel is asked to serve on any sort of committee pertaining to school activities, one may be assured that she'll do her part and do it well. In fact, she is one of the most conscientious class members, never failing in the accomplishments of her aims—whether they be in class work or student activities. Mrs. Merkel's objective is Cornell University, and we feel sure she's going to get there and be successful in mastering the course she's planning to take. Several years before entering our class, Mrs. Merkel attended I. I. & C., after which she was married and came to live in Hattiesburg. But who could live for very long close to a good school like ours without getting interested in it? So Mrs. Merkel decided that before going to Cornell she would graduate from the Normal College.

name: Messer, Otho
description: 'Twas something wiser than fate that gave us the privilege of having him a member of our class. He, with a number of others, left his work in this College and went to answer his country's call in 1917. After more than two years of faithful service, he returned and finished his course here. His qualities we dare not mention, because—well, perhaps too many girls would choose him for their ideal! However, it may be safe to say that, as a rule, he is very congenial. He'd rather say, "You win! You win!" than to argue any day. But wait 'till he's a bit worried, then—"Great Gee!" or "Ah, for Goodness Sake!" He has been a loyal student in every way, ready always to support every phase of College activities. He has made a splendid president of the Athletic Association this year. As a student, he is one of the best, standing high in the estimation of both faculty and students. All in all, he is a MAN—ambitious, successful, deserving, and we unhesitatingly predict for him a great success in any work he may choose.

name: McLendon, George Minor
description: George is an illustration of the saying, "All valuable articles come in small packages." He made a good record in college. He took an active part in athletics, society and Y. M. C. A. work. He was always ready to respond when duty called. The friendly spirit with which he met the students made every one a friend to him. He held many responsible offices while in college.

name: Moore, Richard Curtis
description: Like so many of our great men, he is a "hillbilly." We all prophesy for him a very bright future, because of his studious habits and record at the Normal College. Mr. Moore is one of '20's favorites. Some members held him in awe until recently, when somebody dubbed him "Dicky Boy." Really, "Dicky Boy" sounds more friendly than the name "Mr. Moore." Moore has proved to be an excellent officer in various students' organizations at the Normal College.

name: Pridgen, Alma
description: Alma has proved her ability as a live-wire in all student activities of the Normal College, in that she is now vice-president of the Diploma Class and a member of the Honor Council. She has also taken an active part in other student organizations. She is very reserved in manner but possesses a streak of spice that supplies the "pep" in time of need. She has been spoken of as being distant, but to know her is to admire her and find out her sterling qualities. Wherever she goes to answer her call as a teacher, we take special pride in offering the people a leader and a true representative of the Normal College.

name: Parker, Louisa
nicknames: that brown-eyed girl in the post office
motto: "A perfect woman, nobly planned, To warn, to comfort and to command."
description: We first knew her as "that brown-eyed girl in the post office," but soon we found ourselves telling her the secrets of our hearts, and asking her advice. Her reply always was, "I don't know," but when we left her, we felt much better. She knows the secret of winning friends. Louisa has been one of the most capable and popular girls of our class.

name: Phillips, Kathryn
motto: "A girl as true and sweet and strong as the first wild breath of spring."
description: Kathryn, known to her girl friends as "K. P.", fairly radiates sunshine. The twinkle in her eye, the dimples in her cheeks, and her merry laugh have captured more than one heart. But in spite of her attractiveness, Kathryn is very capable and dignified. She is the only girl who has brains enough to engage constantly in social hour and make the "All A" list every term. Kathryn is studying to be a missionary, but judging from her extreme diligence of late in tatting and other fancy work, we would say that her mission is to be specialized and limited.

name: Ratliff, Willena
description: Our Willena, "pleasingly plump," believes in "doing things freely." She dispenses her smiles and good cheer "freely." She studies "freely"—that is when she studies at all, which is on the way to class. She being endowed with unusual intellect can afford to spend her time in needlework. Wise Willena! She knows "practice makes perfect." Her ambition is to be an artistic Home Science teacher. She goes to Peabody soon after leaving us to begin a course in Home Economics. She is noted for losing things. She lost her suitcase one time and her purse twice, but never once lost her radiant smile nor her sense of humor. Even though she was frantic with horror the time she opened her suitcase and found she had exchanged for one containing "intoxicating drinks," her sense of humor came to her rescue and she thoroughly enjoyed the situation.

name: Wade, Rachel
motto: "Vanish care and exit sorrow. Worry never, always hope; Take today, forget tomorrow. This is 'Wa Wa's' favorite dope."
description: Rachel, better known as "Wa-Wa," hails from Hazlehurst, Mississippi. She was a member of the class that graduated from the H. H. S. in 1918. She has studied hard and smiled her way through M. N. C. Rachel's ambition is to have a pretty little bungalow. Now we know that she is a great lover of beauty and romance. Will this little bungalow be located in some enchanted spot, surrounded by beautiful trees with a quiet "Lane" leading to it? We wonder.

name: Watts, Nannie
description: With a natural antipathy for certain subjects in college, yet an ever-increasing love for others, she lived on day after day. No task assigned ever found in her a wavering spirit, but always self-reliance and self-determination. She is always full of the charming grace and winsome manners that win for every girl the heart of others. In my mind, I now picture her as the possibilities of the future have become realities. Seated at the stenographer's desk in one of the leading firms of the South, she answers fully the question: "What can a girl do?" We are proud of Nannie, very proud, yet this picture is incomplete, for college influences are ever present. Let us leave her then as she is scoring 100 percent in Housekeeping.

name: Ware, Jesse Willis
high school: Agricultural High School of his county
motto: "Be the master of your chances and let God be the Captain of your souls."
clubs: cabinet member, "Y" as director of music
description: Jesse W. Ware discovered America in Walnut Grove, Leake county, Mississippi. He attended the Agricultural High School of his county after finishing the grammar school of his home town. He taught school three years in Leake county before he entered the Mississippi Normal College at Hattiesburg in the fall of 1916. During his second year in college, he joined the navy. After going through the war with distinction, he resumed his course of study where he had left off upon entering the war. That Ware has a pleasant disposition and the confidence of his fellow students was shown by his being elected to the following offices. Ware failed to finish his "Social Hour" course while in college, but has laid the foundation upon which to build later. His favorite subject is Math, and his greatest ambition is to be able to figure out how he may some day ride through Leake county on a railroad.
